coredata.dc:title: Significant improvements in energy density and efficiency of CQDs/PVDF-based dielectric nanocomposite via stretching effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fan
Jiang, Xun
Guo, Ru
Luo, Hang
Wei, Weifeng
Zhang, Dou
coredata.dc:description: With the advancement towards lightweight, integrated, and intelligent capacitors, there is an urgent need to develop flexible dielectric materials with high power density and high energy storage performance. Poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) based nanocomposite has garnered widespread attention due to its high polarization and ease of processing. However, the inverse relationship between the dielectric constant and breakdown strength constrains its potential for achieving high energy density. Herein, a two-pronged approach effectively combining the incorporation of organic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofiller and PVDF polymer crystallization behavior modulation is utilized to boost the energy density of nanocomposites. Particularly, CQDs increase the crystallinity and decrease the leakage current density of the nanocomposites effectively, resulting in enhanced breakdown strength. Moreover, through optimizing process parameters of stretching temperatures and rate during uniaxial stretching, significantly higher crystallinity, polar phase transition, increased chain orientation, and smoother surface are achieved to construct a denser and more stable microstructure that effectively hinders the formation of electrical breakdown pathways, thereby enhancing the electric polarization and breakdown strength of the nanocomposites. As a consequence, the ultrahigh discharge energy density of 30.8 J/cm3 with an efficiency of 74.1 % was yielded at 944.2 kV/mm, representing improvements of 124.8 % and 42.8 %, respectively, compared with pure PVDF (13.7 J/cm3, 51.9 %). These facile uniaxial stretching films present excellent energy storage performance as those obtained by complicated processes, which shows great potential for practical large-scale applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dielectrics
PVDF
Carbon quantum dots
Crystallization behavior
Energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of CQDs content in nanocomposite
Effect of stretching temperatures on structure evolution and electric properties
Effect of stretching rates on structure evolution and electric properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172265), Scientific research project of Hunan Provincial Department of Education (21B0009), Excellent Youth Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ20067), The science and technology innovation Program of Hunan Province (2022RC1074), Central South University Innovation-Driven Research Program (2023CXQD010) and State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China. Dr. Guo thanks to support from the CUHK Postdoctoral fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns, (b) d-space of (110/200) β and the crystallite size, (c) DSC curves, (d) crystallinity, (e) FTIR spectra, (f) calculated phase content percentage of each phase of nanocomposites of the nanocomposites at different stretching temperatures.
(a-f) PFM surface morphology, the samples are placed with the stretching direction up.

(a-f) 2D-WAXD patterns of nanocomposites at different stretching temperatures, (g) diffraction intensity-azimuth curve, (h)the films are placed in the direction of stretching, and the X-ray beam is perpendicular to the film surface.

(a) Weibull distribution, (b) breakdown strength and shape parameter β, (c, d) leakage current density, (e) discharge energy density, (f) energy efficiency of the nanocomposites at different stretching temperatures.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) d-space of (110/200)β and the crystallite size, (c) DSC curves, (d) crystallinity, (e) FTIR spectra, (f) calculated phase content percentage of each phase of nanocomposites of the nanocomposites at different stretching rates.
(a-f) PFM surface morphology, the samples with different stretch rates are placed with the stretching direction up.

(a-g) 2D-WAXD patterns of nanocomposites at different stretching rates, (g) diffraction intensity-azimuth curve, (h) the films are placed in the direction of stretching, and the X-ray beam is perpendicular to the film surface.

(a) Weibull distribution, (b) breakdown strength and shape parameter β, (c, d) leakage current density (inset shows a local magnification of the leakage current density), (e) discharge energy density, (f) energy efficiency of the nanocomposites at different stretching rates.
(a) The radar chart of energy storage performance of PVDF, 0.2D-80 and 0.2D-0.08, (b) Comparison of energy storage performance with other similar works in recent years [18,44–49].

head.ce:title: Significant improvements in energy density and efficiency of CQDs/PVDF-based dielectric nanocomposite via stretching effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan Province, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, N.T., Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, N.T. Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the advancement towards lightweight, integrated, and intelligent capacitors, there is an urgent need to develop flexible dielectric materials with high power density and high energy storage performance. Poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) based nanocomposite has garnered widespread attention due to its high polarization and ease of processing. However, the inverse relationship between the dielectric constant and breakdown strength constrains its potential for achieving high energy density. Herein, a two-pronged approach effectively combining the incorporation of organic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofiller and PVDF polymer crystallization behavior modulation is utilized to boost the energy density of nanocomposites. Particularly, CQDs increase the crystallinity and decrease the leakage current density of the nanocomposites effectively, resulting in enhanced breakdown strength. Moreover, through optimizing process parameters of stretching temperatures and rate during uniaxial stretching, significantly higher crystallinity, polar phase transition, increased chain orientation, and smoother surface are achieved to construct a denser and more stable microstructure that effectively hinders the formation of electrical breakdown pathways, thereby enhancing the electric polarization and breakdown strength of the nanocomposites. As a consequence, the ultrahigh discharge energy density of 30.8 J/cm3 with an efficiency of 74.1 % was yielded at 944.2 kV/mm, representing improvements of 124.8 % and 42.8 %, respectively, compared with pure PVDF (13.7 J/cm3, 51.9 %). These facile uniaxial stretching films present excellent energy storage performance as those obtained by complicated processes, which shows great potential for practical large-scale applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the advancement of the electronics and electrical industry, there is an increasing demand for dielectric materials with higher energy densities to meet the requirements for miniaturization and lightweight design electrostatic capacitors [1–3]. Polymer dielectrics have attracted significant interest in various sectors owing to their advantageous properties, including facile processability, compactness, low weight, superior flexibility, high breakdown strength, and minimal dielectric loss [4–8]. The energy density (U
d ) of dielectric materials is typically expressed as:
U dis
= ∫ EdD . The energy density of the dielectrics is essentially determined by the polarization ( D ) and the electric field ( E ). Achieving high energy density thus necessitates maximizing both the dielectric constant (ε
r ) and the breakdown strength of the dielectrics. Currently, commercially available biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) achieves an energy density of only 1–2 J/cm3, as limited by its low dielectric constant (ɛ r = 2.2), which significantly restricts its practical applications [9,10]. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), as a typical ferroelectric polymer with the strong dipole moment of CF bonds, possesses high relative permittivity (ε r ∼ 10) and high field-induced polarization and is becoming a preferred choice for energy storage capacitors. However, the ferroelectric switching inherent in PVDF results in significant remanent polarization, thereby limiting its energy storage efficiency (η) to approximately 50 % under high electric fields. This substantial inefficiency not only leads to considerable energy wastage but also constrains the improvement of its energy density [11,12].

In recent years, numerous effective strategies have been developed to address the issues of high energy loss and low energy density in PVDF, including molecular chain modification, blending, multilayer architectures, filler/polymer nanocomposites, irradiation, and stretching or pressing techniques. [13–16]. Adding nanofillers into polymers is a proven approach to enhancing the energy density of nanocomposites. Ryu et al. [17] introduced Sr1.8Bi0.2Nb3O10 (SBNO) nanosheets into PVDF and constructed a multilayered nanocomposite (PVDF/SBNO/PVDF). Through the barrier effect from 2D ultrathin (14 nm) and highly aligned SBNO nanosheets, the energy storage density of the composite reached 12.8 J/cm3. Additionally, Zhu et al. [18] developed a novel metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived Fe as nanofiller and utilized its created micro-capacitance and interfacial polarization effects to enhance the overall polarization strength of the nanocomposite. This combined effect allowed the nanocomposite to achieve a high dielectric constant (ε r = 15.3) and breakdown strength (617.1 kV/mm) at low filler content, resulting in a significantly increased energy storage density of 28.9 J/cm3. Although notable improvements have been made by these efforts, owing to the dielectric mismatch and particle agglomeration, the interfacial microregion between these inorganic ceramic nanofillers and polymer matrix remains a wicked issue. The common-existing interfacial incompatibility more often leads to a premature breakdown and charge leakage inside the nanocomposite, which poses the inverse relationship between the dielectric constant and breakdown strength, largely limiting further breakthroughs in energy density [19,20].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a newly rising carbon-based nanomaterial have garnered wide interest in the field of optoelectronic devices, bioimaging and energy storage applications benefitting from their great solubility, environmental friendliness, strong photoluminescence emission, and broad-band light absorption. With the unique virtue of all-organic carbon hybrid structure, ultrasmall size (less than 10 nm), and abundant surface functional groups, CQDs are expected to serve as functional additives to effectively resolve the difficulty of interfacial compatibility between nanofillers and polymers [21–23]. Besides, the coulomb-blockade effect of CQDs was verified to enhance the breakdown strength of nanocomposites by acting as an electronic barrier to reducing the injection of extraneous charges and the leakage current, thus the PEI-based nanocomposite achieving an energy density of 4.1 J/cm3 at 200 °C with an efficiency of 90 % [24]. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that introducing all-organic CQDs in PVDF polymer could form a tightly compatible interface microregion to enhance interface polarization while maintaining high breakdown strength.

Apart from the nanofiller-polymer nanocomposites strategy, developing mechanical and physical methods to adjust the microstructure and orientation of polymer matrix have been proven promising approaches to boost energy storage performance [25,26]. With its semi-crystalline nature, PVDF polymer exhibits a complex structure with multiple crystalline polymorphs (α, β, γ, δ, and ε phase) along with the existence of amorphous regions. Among these, the α-phase adopts a trans-gauche-trans-gauche’ (TGTG’) conformation with lower polarity. In contrast, the β phase, characterized by an all-trans (TTTT) conformation, features uniformly aligned dipole moments and substantial ferroelectric domains [27]. Their energy storage capabilities are critically influenced by their microstructure, including crystalline phase, grain size, crystallinity, and chain orientation [25,28–31]. Yan et al. [32] fabricated PVDF films with an exceptionally high β-phase content using a repeated pressing-and-folding technique, which exhibited relaxor-ferroelectric behavior, achieving a high energy storage density of 35 J/cm3 and an efficiency of 74 %. However, this process imposes certain limitations on film thickness and dimensions, particularly when producing larger or thicker films, preserving the original electrical properties can become difficult. Up to now, mechanical stretching is believed to be one of the most promising and scalable processes for manufacturing capacitor films. Guo et al. [26] found that high stretching ratios induced phase transition and Young's modulus enhancement can synergistically improve the dielectric constant and electric breakdown strength of PVDF polymer, indicating the great potential of the uniaxial stretching approach for elevating energy density. However, apart from the stretching strain focused on by previous research, some important process parameters such as stretching temperatures and rate, also significantly affect the thermodynamic and kinetic behavior of PVDF polymer crystallization, thereby determining the dielectric properties and breakdown resistance capability. Therefore, it is necessary to explore more optimal process parameters to fully clarify the microstructure evolution and greatly leverage the nanocomposite's electrical properties for a breakthrough in energy storage performance.

Thereupon, this work developed a two-pronged approach, that effectively combines the addition of organic CQDs nanofiller and PVDF polymer crystallization behavior modulation to sort out the contradictory relationship between electric polarization and the electric breakdown strength, thus boosting the energy storage performance of dielectric nanocomposites. Firstly, an optimal CQD content was obtained to enhance breakdown strength by utilizing its virtue of inhibiting carrier motion and reducing leakage current. Furthermore, the effect of stretching temperatures and rate on the crystalline structure including crystallinity, phase transition, chain orientation, and surface morphology, etc., was systematically investigated. Particularly, these microstructure structure and high-field dielectric properties relationships were carefully clarified. With the process parameter optimization and crystallization behavior tuning, the 0.2 wt% CQDs/PVDF nanocomposite under the stretching temperature and rate of 80 °C and 0.08 mm/s (0.2D-80/0.08) ultimately achieved an ultrahigh breakdown strength and energy density of 944.2 kV/mm and 30.8 J/cm3, representing increases of 43.1 % and 108.1 % compared to the original nanocomposite, respectively. This work revealed microstructural changes underlying dielectric behaviors and indicated great potential of the crystallization behavior modulation of ferroelectric polymers for high-energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CQDs content in nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To begin with, the dispersibility of CQDs in the PVDF polymer matrix was characterized by laser confocal scanning microscopy. As is known, CQDs possess excellent photoluminescent features that emit certain light under different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. S2 (b, d), under the excitation wavelengths of 488 nm and 458 nm, the 0.2D nanocomposite demonstrates uniformly distributed green and yellow light respectively, compared with the black background without fluorescence for pristine PVDF film in Fig. S2 (a, c), which confirmed the even dispersion of CQDs within PVDF without any aggregation. And PVDF nanocomposites with different concentrations of CQDs were prepared, and their dielectric, ferroelectric, leakage current and energy storage properties were compared, as shown in Figs. S3-S6, respectively. As the CQDs filler content increased, the dielectric constant of the nanocomposites also increased. At 650 kV/mm, 0.2D achieved an energy density of 18.9 J/cm3, significantly higher than that of pure PVDF (13.7 J/cm3). This improvement is primarily attributed to the optimal CQDs content effectively inhibiting carrier motion, alleviating local electric field concentration, reducing leakage current, and thereby enhancing breakdown strength. Consequently, the optimal CQDs filler content was determined to be 0.2 wt%. As a whole, the addition of a small number of CQDs still has minimal impact on the high filed polarization of PVDF, limiting its energy density and efficiency (Fig. S4). Hence, the 0.2 wt% CQDs/PVDF nanocomposite was employed for subsequent investigation of the uniaxial stretching process with different temperatures and rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of stretching temperatures on structure evolution and electric properties
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, different stretching temperatures (from 60 to 100 °C, rate of 0.05 mm/s) on the 0.2 wt% CQDs/PVDF nanocomposite were executed. The XRD results in Fig. 1(a) show an obvious crystalline phase transition in nanocomposites. For the unstretched PVDF (P) and 0.2D films, the structure remains predominantly amorphous, with broad and diffuse XRD peaks. At 18.4° and 38.8°, peaks corresponding to the α phase (020) and (002) crystal planes are observed, respectively, along with a peak at 20.4° corresponding to the β phase (110/200) crystal plane. When the 0.2D films were stretched at different temperatures of 60, 80, and 100 °C, respectively (defined as 0.2D-60, 0.2D-80, 0.2D-100), the peaks corresponding to the α phase significantly weakened or even disappeared, while the peaks corresponding to the β phase (110/200) became sharper, indicating enhanced crystallinity of the nanocomposites. This enhancement is attributed to mechanical stress aligning the polymer molecular chains along the direction of stress, causing disentanglement and straightening from their original coiled form, thus leading to a more ordered arrangement. The conformational changes in the polymer chains promote the transition from the loosely packed α phase to the more compact β phase [33]. Besides, using Formulas (1) and (2) in the SI, the d-spacing and crystallite size of the nanocomposites at different stretching temperatures were calculated, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The incorporation of CQDs led to a slight reduction in the (110/200) β spacing and crystallite size, decreasing the crystallite size from 12.0 nm to 11.2 nm. Particularly, the effect of uniaxial stretching was even more pronounced, with a crystallite size reduction to 8.7 nm at a stretching temperature of 60 °C, representing a decrease of 27.5 % and 22.3 % compared to pure PVDF and 0.2D, respectively. Besides, the addition of CQDs reduced the interchain spacing from 4.38 Å to 4.36 Å, and with increasing stretching temperature, the spacing further decreased from 4.36 Å to 4.33 Å. This is because the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs facilitated hydrogen bonding and other interactions with the matrix, promoting tighter molecular chain alignment under stress [34,35]. The original large crystallites were fragmented into smaller parts with more tightly packed molecular chains under stress, which would be beneficial to the increase in breakdown strength.

Then, the impact of the stretching temperature on the crystallinity of nanocomposites was analyzed through DSC curves, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The stress-induced fragmentation and refinement of large crystallites resulted in a shorter melting range, and the DSC curves of the uniaxially stretched nanocomposites became significantly sharper, indicating a beneficial effect of uniaxial stretching on crystallinity enhancement. Using Formula (3) from the Supplementary data, the crystallinity of the nanocomposites was calculated and shown in Fig. 1(d). With increasing stretching temperature, crystallinity initially rises and then declines, which can be explained by the following reasons: At lower temperatures, the limited mobility of molecular chains results in less orderly arrangements. As the temperature increases, the mobility and orientation of the chains improve, promoting crystallization. However, excessive thermal motion leads to increased disorder, hindering crystallinity at even higher temperatures. Additionally, CQDs slightly enhance the crystallization capability of PVDF, acting similarly to heterogeneous nucleating agents. The highest crystallinity (74.7 %) is achieved at a stretching temperature of 80 °C, representing increases of 47.0 % and 44.7 % compared to pure PVDF and 0.2D, respectively.

Moreover, FTIR spectra were employed for quantitative analysis of changes in the crystalline phase content of the nanocomposites. In Fig. 1(e), the peaks corresponding to non-polar α phases in the stretched films weaken or disappear, while those corresponding to polar β phases significantly intensify. This indicates that uniaxial stretching promotes phase transitions within the nanocomposites, generating more polar phases favorable for enhanced energy density. Using the FTIR spectra and Formulas (4-7) from the Supplementary data, the phase content was calculated (Fig. 1(f)). With increasing stretching temperature, the polar phase content initially increases and then decreases. Moderate temperature can enhance chain mobility to facilitate the transition to polar phases. However, further temperature increases lead to greater molecular chain relaxation, favoring the maintenance of the α phase. At the optimal stretching temperature of 80 °C, the polar phase content peaks at 86 %, where β and γ phase account for 49.1 % and 36.9 %, respectively, while the α phase content reduces to below half of its original value (14 %).

PFM analysis was conducted to investigate the influence of stretching temperature on the surface morphology and roughness of nanocomposites. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , at lower stretching temperatures of 60 °C, the limited mobility of molecular chains results in partial chain alignment along the stretching direction, with the orientation process predominantly causing fragmentation. When the temperature rises to 80 °C, the enhanced chain mobility leads to a significant increase in overall orientation, with the deformation of voids manifesting as extended ellipsoids or even linear forms. Further temperature increases (100 °C) enable coordinated chain segment motion, completing the overall molecular alignment, yet thermal motion induces disorder, reducing the degree of orientation and fragmentation. PFM results confirm that an optimal stretching temperature evidently enhances molecular chain alignment and significantly reduces the surface roughness of the nanocomposite films. With the increase in stretching temperature, the surface roughness of the nanocomposites first decreases and then increases, with the 0.2D-80 sample exhibiting a minimum surface roughness of only 41.2 nm. Additionally, the excellent interfacial compatibility between CQDs and PVDF is benefits in promoting molecular chain mobility, further reducing surface roughness.

A clear representation of the crystallographic orientation within the nanocomposites was characterized by 2D-WAXD analysis. In Fig. 3 , the 2D-WAXD patterns of the unstretched samples exhibit diffuse and uniform rings, indicating random orientation within the material. After uniaxial stretching, the (110/200) β arcs become narrower and significantly more intense, suggesting that the β phase crystals are almost perpendicular to the stretching direction. This implies that stretching effectively promotes the orientation of β phase crystallization, aligning the c-axis (polymer carbon chains) nearly parallel to the polymer surface. Meanwhile, the dipoles are naturally distributed in the plane parallel to the electric field, which is conducive to enhancing their responsiveness to the electric field. As the stretching temperature increases to 100 °C, the arc length in the 2D-WAXD patterns extends, indicating a lower degree of orientation, corresponding to the PFM results. The azimuthal scan profiles were obtained from the scattering intensities integrated azimuthally at a q value of (110,200) β reflections. As shown in Fig. 3(g), all present a strong peak with azimuth angle changing except unstretched P and 0.2D, which demonstrates that β phase in P and 0.2D has no preferred orientation. By contrast, all the peaks of stretched films are located at 180°, meaning that the β phase orientates along with the stretching direction. Besides, the peak intensity of 0.2D-80 is stronger than others, illustrating its better orientation of the β phase.

The low-field dielectric property as a function of frequency for the nanocomposites was further analyzed. In Fig. S7, as the frequency increases, the dielectric constant decreases while the dielectric loss increases, showing a characteristic of dielectric relaxation phenomena [19]. The incorporation of CQDs slightly enhances the dielectric constant of the nanocomposites, which is mainly due to interfacial polarization induced by polymer dots within the PVDF matrix. For the stretched films, the dielectric constant presents a trend of rising and then falling as the stretching temperature increases, with the maximum value obtained at 80 °C. This is attributed to the higher degree of orientation in 0.2D-80, which enhances its dipole rotation response capability.

Then, the high-field polarization was analyzed by the electric polarization-electric field loop (P-E loop). Under the same electric field, the stretched films exhibit higher polarization intensity due to the increased polar phase content (Fig. S8). Specifically, 0.2D-80 achieves the highest electric polarization, benefiting from its high crystallinity, orientation degree, and polar phase content. Besides, I-E curves were tested to investigate the field-induced current behavior of films with different stretching temperatures (Fig. S9). During the charge-discharge cycles at a 300 kV/mm electric field, no significant current peak was observed for P and 0.2D. Additionally, a qualitative analysis of the high-field dielectric constant was performed using the formula
ε r
=
∂ D / ∂ E
/
ε 0
. The derivative of D to E for the charging-discharging process at different electric fields is illustrated in Fig. S10, where the peak intensity reflects the ease of dipole alignment with the electric field [36]. All stretched films present a peak around 130 kV/mm when the external electric field is above 200 kV/mm, which means their fastest switching field is lowered compared to the 250 kV/mm for pure PVDF and 320 kV/mm for 0.2D, respectively. This is because polymer chains of stretched films were mostly parallel to the polymer surface, making dipoles distributed randomly on the plane perpendicular to the electric field, which is beneficial for them to respond to the external electric field rapidly. On the other hand, peak amplitude reflects the number of switchable dipoles. The peak of 0.2D-80 decreases less with increasing electric field, indicating the more reversible polar structure inside than those unstretched and stretched at 60/100 °C.

Next, the electric breakdown behavior of nanocomposites was analyzed through a two-parameter Weibull distribution. As shown in Fig. 4 (a, b), the incorporation of an optimal amount of CQDs significantly enhances the breakdown field (
E b
) of the nanocomposites. This is because CQDs act as traps to effectively confine and restrict carrier motion, thereby reducing leakage current density and increasing the breakdown strength of PVDF from 513.9 kV/mm to 615.9 kV/mm. It is worth mentioning that the breakdown strength of the stretched films further largely increases after stretching at different temperatures, which was contributed from the following aspects: First, the uniaxial stretching aligns the molecular chains perpendicular to the electric field, which is more advantageous in hindering electrical treeing compared to random or parallel alignment, thereby extending the breakdown path and enhancing
E b
[37]. Moreover, the increase in crystallinity induces a counteracting depolarization field at the crystalline regions, further improving breakdown resistance. Specifically, as the stretching temperature increases, the nanocomposite's crystallinity and degree of orientation initially rise and then fall, while the surface roughness decreases first and then increases. Consequently, the highest breakdown strength of 819.1 kV/mm was achieved in the nanocomposite with the stretching temperature of 80 °C, showing a 60.3 % improvement over PVDF. In Fig. 4 (c, d), the leakage current density at 250 kV/mm indicates that CQDs moderately reduce leakage current density. The significantly suppressed leakage current is even more pronounced in stretched films. The tighter molecular packing reduces the free volume of the polymer, creating barriers to the accumulation or movement of space charges, thereby restricting carrier motion. Additionally, the perpendicular orientation of molecular chains to the electric field further extends the carrier path and reduces leakage current. As the temperature increases, the leakage current density first decreases and then increases. High-ratio stretching at either too low or too high temperatures tends to induce microcracks and voids to increase electric field concentration and the likelihood of forming carrier pathways, finally leading to increased conductivity loss, decreased breakdown strength, and reduced energy density.

Last, the energy density and efficiency of the nanocomposites were calculated according to Fig. S8, as shown in Fig. 4(e, f). The energy density of all films gradually elevates with increasing electric field, while energy efficiency presents the trend of first decreases at a relatively low electric field and followed by an increase with further advancing electric field. The first declined energy efficiency is attributed to a field-induced phase transition from nonpolar α to polar β phase. It is difficult for these dipoles to revert to their original states during the discharging process, resulting in significant residual polarization and energy loss. After reaching the polarization saturation, more newly reversible dipoles generated at a higher electric field are devoted to the discharging energy density quickly, thus leading to the energy efficiency rising again. By uniaxial stretching, the responsiveness of the dipole in PVDF with the electric field is significantly enhanced, the dipole switching rate is accelerated, and the polarization saturation electric field is significantly reduced. It was observed that the polarization saturation point of the stretched films occurs earlier at around 150 kV/mm compared to the 350 kV/mm and 450 kV/mm for unstretched P and 0.2D films, respectively, bringing an evident advantage of energy density and efficiency in the higher field for stretched films [29]. Furthermore, as the stretching temperature increases, the energy efficiency gradually improves. At a stretching temperature of 60 °C, the efficiency remains above 60 %, and at 100 °C, it reaches 70 %. The nanocomposite uniaxially stretched at 80 °C exhibits the highest energy efficiency, achieving 70.5 % under 800 kV/mm. Ultimately, the 0.2D-80 sample, benefiting from the highest breakdown strength and superior polarization, demonstrates the best energy storage performance, with an energy density and efficiency of 25.4 J/cm3 and 70.5 %, respectively, representing improvements of 135.2 % and 35.6 % compared to pure PVDF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of stretching rates on structure evolution and electric properties
ce:section.ce:para: The stretching rate is another crucial factor to influence the microstructure and performance of nanocomposite films. Based on the above optimal stretching temperature (80 °C) and stretching ratio of 4. Different stretching rates (from 0.05 to 0.12 mm/s) on the nanocomposite have been investigated. Firstly, XRD patterns of nanocomposites were present in Fig. 5 (a, b). It is evident that with increasing stretching rate, both the crystallite size and molecular chain spacing initially decrease and then increase. As known, the molecular chains must overcome internal viscous resistance during orientation [38]. When the stretching rate is too fast, the movement of molecular chains significantly lags, leading to a decrease in orientation degree and reduced fragmentation of crystals. Conversely, when the stretching rate is too slow, the molecular chains tend to relax and retract quickly upon unloading, causing reorientation and curling, which is detrimental to alignment and crystallization fragmentation. Then, DSC curves were employed to analyze the crystallization behavior of the nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 5(c, d). With increasing stretching rate, the crystallinity of the nanocomposites first increases and then decreases. At a stretching rate of 0.08 mm/s (0.2D-0.08), the nanocomposites exhibit the highest crystallinity, reaching 70.7 %. Besides, FTIR results in Fig. 5(e, f) showed the increase in stretching speed weakly suppressed the phase transition from α to polar (β / γ) phases, also indicating a relaxation process for the transition of molecular conformation.

The surface morphology and roughness of nanocomposites subjected to varying stretching rates were characterized using PFM, as shown in Fig. 6 . It reveals that uniaxial stretching significantly enhances orientation intensity. At lower stretching rates, partial orientation along the stretching direction is observed, with oriented fibrous structures remaining relatively coarse and limited fragmentation. As the stretching rate increases, these fibrous structures become more extended, indicating enhanced orientation and significant fragmentation. However, at excessively high stretching rates, the mobility of molecular chains becomes restricted, causing the molecular chains to lag behind the strain changes, thereby reducing orientation. Particularly, when the stretching rate reaches 0.12 mm/s, the PVDF exhibits the weakest orientation nearly without fibrous morphology. Besides, the surface roughness of the nanocomposites initially decreases and then increases with the stretching rate. 0.2D-0.08 achieves the lowest surface roughness (21.7 nm), which is a substantial reduction compared to 0.2D (117 nm).

2D-WAXD analysis was conducted to verify the orientation of the crystalline structures within the nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 7 . The narrowing and intensified arcs of the (110/200)β indicate that the β-phase crystals are nearly perpendicular to the stretching direction [23]. As the stretching rate increases, the relaxation of molecular chain movements decreases, making it more challenging to achieve stable α phase crystalline orientation. The azimuthal scan profiles were obtained from the scattering intensities integrated azimuthally at a q value of (110, 200)β reflections. Similarly, all the stretched films show strong peaks in Fig. 7(g), due to the better phase orientation induced by uniaxial stretching. 0.2D-0.08 presents a stronger peak, which means its higher orientation degree of β phase.

Fig. 8 (a, b) illustrates the Weibull distribution and breakdown strength of nanocomposites at different stretching rates. As the stretching rate increases, the breakdown strength of the nanocomposites initially rises and then declines, while the β value remains at a high level. This behavior can be attributed to the fact that an appropriate stretching rate effectively enhances the crystallinity of the nanocomposites, promoting the formation of depolarization electric fields at crystalline regions and alleviating local field concentration [40]. Furthermore, the increased orientation degree induces the movement of charge carriers along the oriented interfaces, thus extending the development path of electrical trees and impeding breakdown [41]. Consequently, the 0.2D-0.08 nanocomposite exhibits a breakdown strength of up to 944.2 kV/mm while maintaining a high β value of 28.5. However, excessively high stretching rates are detrimental to both crystallinity and molecular orientation, leading to defects such as voids. These defects cause significant leakage currents and localized electric field concentrations under an external electric field, thereby limiting the breakdown strength. Moreover, the leakage current curve in Fig. 8(c, d) further confirms the changes in
E b
. The reduction of leakage current helps prevent premature thermal breakdown caused by Joule heating, thereby enhancing the breakdown strength [42]. The inset reveals that lower strain rates favor lower leakage current, albeit with subtle variations. Generally accepted, tighter molecular packing reduces the free volume within the polymer, increasing resistance to space charge accumulation or movement. Besides, molecular chains oriented perpendicular to the electric field direction also impedes charge carrier mobility, effectively reducing leakage current [43]. As the stretching rate increases, the crystallinity and molecular orientation of the nanocomposites initially rise and then decline, resulting in an initial decrease and then an increase in leakage current density. The 0.2D-0.08 exhibits the lowest leakage current density, also accounting for the highest breakdown strength yield in this film. Last, the energy density and efficiency of the nanocomposites were calculated according to the P-E loops (Fig. S12), as shown in Fig. 8(e, f). Obviously, the stretched films demonstrate largely improved energy density and efficiency than that of unstretched film. Moreover, with increasing stretching rates, both the energy density and efficiency of the nanocomposites exhibit trends of initially rising and then declining, which is similar to the breakdown strength. As a consequence, at a stretching rate of 0.08 mm/s, the optimal energy performance is achieved in 0.2D-0.08 nanocomposite film, with an energy density of 30.8 J/cm3 and an efficiency of 74.1 %, representing improvements of 124.82 % and 42.77 %, respectively, as comparison with pure PVDF (13.7 J/cm3, 51.9 %).

Finally, the overall performance involving key indicators E
b ,
P max
−
P r
, ε
r , U
d , η, of different nanocomposites was summarized and compared in Fig. 9(a). E
b is the electric field strength at breakdown. A substantial improvement was obtained in 0.2D-0.08 nanocomposite as compared with pure PVDF and 0.2D-80 nanocomposite, representing the effectiveness of process parameter optimization. Moreover, 0.2D-0.08 nanocomposite exhibits the largest area and best overall energy storage performance, with an ultrahigh energy density of 30.8 J/cm3 and an efficiency of 74.1 %. This work identifies the optimal stretching process to achieve high energy storage performance, demonstrating significant advantages in energy density and efficiency compared to similar works in recent years.

In summary, a novel all-organic filler carbon quantum dot (CQD) was incorporated and a facile uniaxial stretching process was utilized in this work to improve the energy density of PVDF nanocomposites. The ultrasmall and organic CQDs nanofiller increase the crystallinity and decrease the leakage current of nanocomposites, which is beneficial for hindering charges from leaping and prolonging the electrical tree path to improve the breakdown strength. A discharge energy density of 18.8 J/cm3 has been achieved, which is improved by 38.0 % compared with pure PVDF. Further, the effect of stretching temperatures and rate on the microstructure and electrical properties was systematically investigated. Through process parameter optimization, the higher crystallinity, polar phase transition, increased chain orientation, and smoother surface were achieved to construct a denser and more stable microstructure that effectively hinders the formation of electrical breakdown pathways, thereby enhancing the electric polarization and breakdown strength of the nanocomposites. As a result, the 0.2 wt% CQDs/PVDF nanocomposite stretched under the temperature of 80 °C and rate of 0.08 mm/s present the best energy storage performance with discharge energy density of 30.8 J/cm3 and energy efficiency of 74.1 %. This work not only reveals microstructure changes underlying dielectric behaviors but also obtains as good energy storage performance as those obtained by complicated processes via a facile uniaxial stretching process, demonstrating great potential for practical large-scale applications.

Fan Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xun Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis. Ru Guo: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Hang Luo: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Weifeng Wei: Resources. Dou Zhang: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172265), Scientific research project of Hunan Provincial Department of Education (21B0009), Excellent Youth Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ20067), The science and technology innovation Program of Hunan Province (2022RC1074), Central South University Innovation-Driven Research Program (2023CXQD010) and State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha, China. Dr. Guo thanks to support from the CUHK Postdoctoral fellowship.
